exercises.title: A: Error correction
exercises.instructions: Each of the numbered words or phrases in bold is incorrect. Rewrite them correctly. Write - if the bold word or phrase simply needs to be removed.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each of the numbered words or phrases in bold is incorrect. Rewrite them correctly. Write - if the bold word or phrase simply needs to be removed.

items.sentence: Renewable energy might help to us cut our carbon footprint, but imagine if we could completely out eliminate fossil fuels and stop pollution from in the first place.
items.explanation: Help **us** (not to us); remove **out**; stop pollution **happening** (not from).

items.sentence: Reversing climate change may be the greatest scientific challenge of our era. For a long of time, experts have debated whether global action is even possible, but reaching a binding international agreement has proved far harder than they expected that.
items.explanation: Fixed phrase → **long time** (not long of time); remove unnecessary **that**.

items.sentence: The global climate system is incredibly complex, with rising temperatures having a direct impact taken on ecosystems all over the world.
items.explanation: Redundant word → remove **taken** (impact on is enough).

items.sentence: The researchers may feel they are wasting up valuable resources, but a breakthrough could transform the way we understand our planet.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: B: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals
exercises.title: B: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.

items.sentence: The weatherman said there was a high (LIKELY) ...... of storms this weekend.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from LIKELY, so the correct answer is "likelihood".

wrongOptions.likely: The root LIKELY cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "likelihood".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of LIKELY that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: LIKELY → likelihood: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "likelihood" is the word-formation answer from the root LIKELY in this context.

items.sentence: Everyone knows that driving cars produces (POLLUTE) ......, yet millions of us still choose to drive every day.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from POLLUTE, so the correct answer is "pollution".

wrongOptions.pollute: The root POLLUTE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "pollution".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of POLLUTE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: POLLUTE → pollution: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "pollution" is the word-formation answer from the root POLLUTE in this context.

items.sentence: Predicting the effects of climate change (ACCURATE) ...... requires a great deal of scientific expertise.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is adverb modifying requires, so the correct answer is "accurately".

wrongOptions.accurate: The root ACCURATE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "accurately".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of ACCURATE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: ACCURATE → accurately: Use the -ing form for an activity or gerund.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "accurately" is the word-formation answer from the root ACCURATE in this context.

wrongOptions.resident: The root RESIDENT cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "residential".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of RESIDENT that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: RESIDENT → residential: Change the word class to an adjective with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "residential" is the word-formation answer from the root RESIDENT in this context.

wrongOptions.environment: The root ENVIRONMENT cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "environmentalists".

explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: ENVIRONMENT → environmentalists: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "environmentalists" is the word-formation answer from the root ENVIRONMENT in this context.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is weather adjective, so the correct answer is "sunny".

wrongOptions.sun: The root SUN cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "sunny".

explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.

wrongOptions.danger: The root DANGER cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "endangered".

explanationContent.wordFormation: DANGER → endangered: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: C: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.title: C: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using the word given so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using the word given so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
Did you see the nature documentary about the Amazon rainforest? **saw**
I wonder ...... the nature documentary about the Amazon rainforest.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is reported question, so the correct answer is "if you saw".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "if you saw", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Indirect speech keeps statement word order — do not invert subject and auxiliary.

items.sentence:
I spotted a deer for a moment before it vanished into the trees. **sight**
I ...... a deer before it vanished into the trees.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is fixed phrase, so the correct answer is "caught sight of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "caught sight of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "caught sight of" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

items.sentence:
I hope the weather improves for our hiking trip to the lake district this weekend. **up**
I hope ...... for our hiking trip to the lake district this weekend.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is weather phrasal verb, so the correct answer is "the weather clears up".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "the weather clears up", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
Did you hear that the old windmill on the hill had been demolished? **torn**
Did you hear that they ...... the old windmill on the hill?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is demolish, so the correct answer is "had torn down".

wrongOptions.torn down: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "had torn down", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence:
Everything we do has an impact on the natural world in one way or another. **effect**
Everything we do ...... the natural world in one way or another.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is collocation, so the correct answer is "has an effect on".

wrongOptions.an effect on: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
I don't really know much about the work that the WWF does. **familiar**
I ...... the work that the WWF does.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is familiar with, so the correct answer is "am not really familiar with".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "am not really familiar with", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
The developers have ruined the local park. **mess**
The developers have ...... the local park.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is ruin idiom, so the correct answer is "made a mess of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "made a mess of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
The expert said the problems were caused by pollution from the factory. **put**
The expert ...... pollution from the factory.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is attribute cause, so the correct answer is "put the problems down to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "put the problems down to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence:
Most people know that wasting energy contributes to climate change. **aware**
Most people ...... the contribution of wasting energy to climate change.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is aware of, so the correct answer is "are aware of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "are aware of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.
